sections.text: Welcome back. Algebra One, Module Eleven — systems of linear equations. The skill that turns word problems with two unknowns into two simple equations you can actually solve. Let's get into it.

Here's a real one. A movie theater sold fourteen tickets for one hundred twenty-eight dollars. Adult tickets are twelve dollars. Child tickets are seven dollars. How many of each did they sell? Two unknowns — adults and children. You cannot solve this with one equation. You need two. That's a system of equations, and that's what today is about.

Four things to cover. One — what a system of equations actually is. Two — the graphing method. Three — substitution. Four — elimination. Plus, how to pick the right method for the problem in front of you.

A system of equations is just two or more equations sharing the same variables. And the solution is the pair of numbers — usually written as x comma y — that makes ALL the equations true at the same time. Not one. All of them. That's the whole game.

Method one — graphing. Plot both lines on the coordinate plane. Wherever they cross, that point is your solution. Here, y equals x plus one and y equals negative x plus five cross at two comma three. The catch — graphing works, but it's slow, and the answer is only exact when the intersection lands cleanly on grid lines. Most of the time, we want algebra.

Method two — substitution. Solve one equation for one variable. Then plug that expression into the OTHER equation. Now you've got one equation with one variable, and you can solve it. Substitution is the right choice when one variable is already isolated — like when an equation already starts with y equals.

Let's do one. y equals two x plus one. And x plus y equals ten. The first equation already gives us y, so we substitute. x plus the quantity two x plus one equals ten. That simplifies to three x plus one equals ten. So x equals three. Plug back in — y equals two times three plus one, which is seven. The solution is three comma seven.

Method three — elimination. Add or subtract the two equations so one variable cancels out. Sometimes you need to multiply one equation first to make the coefficients match. Elimination shines when the coefficients are already lined up — or are easy to line up with a quick multiplication.

Watch this. Two x plus three y equals twelve. And four x minus three y equals six. The y coefficients are already opposites — three and negative three. So just ADD the equations. The y's cancel. We get six x equals eighteen, so x equals three. Plug back in — two times three plus three y equals twelve, giving y equals two. Solution — three comma two.
Your turn. Solve this system — x plus y equals ten, and two x minus y equals two. Hint — try elimination. Add the equations to cancel the y. Pause now. Solve it. Press play when you're ready.

Time. Add the equations — the y's cancel and you get three x equals twelve, so x equals four. Then four plus y equals ten, which gives y equals six. The solution is four comma six. If you got that, you've got the method.

Quick but important. Not every system has a single answer. Three possible outcomes. One — the lines cross at one point, one solution. Two — the lines are parallel, same slope, different intercept, they never meet, NO solutions. Three — the two equations are actually the same line, infinite solutions. Recognizing these three cases saves you from chasing a solution that doesn't exist.

Back to the movie theater. Fourteen tickets, one hundred twenty-eight dollars total. Adults are twelve dollars, kids are seven. Let a be the number of adults, c be the number of children. Equation one — a plus c equals fourteen, the total tickets. Equation two — twelve a plus seven c equals one hundred twenty-eight, the total dollars. Two equations, two unknowns. A system.

Substitution time. From equation one, a equals fourteen minus c. Plug into equation two — twelve times the quantity fourteen minus c, plus seven c, equals one hundred twenty-eight. Distribute — one hundred sixty-eight minus five c equals one hundred twenty-eight. So c equals eight. Then a equals fourteen minus eight, which is six. Six adults and eight children. Done.

How to pick a method. If one variable is already isolated — y equals something — use substitution. If the coefficients are easy to match or already cancel, use elimination. Graphing is mostly for building intuition or for problems where the numbers are friendly. Substitution and elimination are your everyday tools.

Recap. A system of equations is two or more equations with the same variables. Three methods — graphing, substitution, elimination. Three possible outcomes — one solution, no solutions, or infinite solutions. And word problems become systems when you assign variables and write down what you know. That last part is the real skill.

This video is the overview. To actually master systems, here's the rest. Read OpenStax Chapter Four on Systems of Linear Equations. Run the Khan Academy systems of equations practice — full set. Do the assignment in your dashboard — solve four systems with the method of your choice, plus one word problem. Book an advisor check-in if any of it feels fuzzy. Next up — Module Twelve — exponents and polynomials.
